blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلطُّورِ (١)
By the Mount [Sinai], (1)
وَكِتَـٰبٍ مَّسْطُورٍ (٢)
by the Book inscribed (2)
فِى رَقٍّ مَّنشُورٍ (٣)
on an unrolled parchment; (3)
وَٱلْبَيْتِ ٱلْمَعْمُورِ (٤)
by the House greatly frequented; (4)
وَٱلسَّقْفِ ٱلْمَرْفُوعِ (٥)
by the vault raised high, (5)
وَٱلْبَحْرِ ٱلْمَسْجُورِ (٦)
by the surging sea: (6)
إِنَّ عَذَابَ رَبِّكَ لَوَٰقِعٌ (٧)
indeed your Lord’s punishment will surely befall. (7)
مَّا لَهُۥ مِن دَافِعٍ (٨)
There is none who can avert it. (8)
يَوْمَ تَمُورُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ مَوْرًا (٩)
On the day when the sky whirls violently, (9)
وَتَسِيرُ ٱلْجِبَالُ سَيْرًا (١٠)
and the mountains move with an awful motion: (10)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to warn the people of denial and obstinacy among the disbelievers (الكفار) of the punishment prepared for them on the Day of Resurrection. It begins by announcing the occurrence of the punishment they were warned of and its realization on the Day of Resurrection through emphasized oaths and solemn swearing, asserting that it shall not leave them on that day until it befalls them, and there is no escape.

Then, it mentions a portion of the description of this punishment and the woe that will encompass them and never leave them. Subsequently, it contrasts this with a glimpse of the bliss of the people of delight on that day—the God-conscious (المتقون) — those who maintain an inner state of vigilance and protection against Allah’s displeasure — who were in the world apprehensive among their folk, supplicating to Allah, believing in Him, and professing His oneness (موحدين له).

Next, it proceeds to rebuke the deniers for what they used to charge the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with, and for their rejection of what was revealed to him of the Quran and the true religion (الدين الحق) he brought.

The discourse concludes by repeating the threat and the promise of punishment, and by commanding the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to glorify his Lord. The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالطُّورِ By the Mount, It has been said that al-Tur (الطور) refers to any mountain in general, but its usage has become predominant for the mountain upon which Allah spoke to Moses عليه‌السلام. It is more appropriate that the intended meaning in the verse is the Mount of Moses عليه‌السلام; Allah the Exalted swore by it because He sanctified it and blessed it, just as He swore by it in His saying: وَطُورِ سِينِينَ and Mount Sinai, (95:2). He also said: وَنادَيْناهُ مِنْ جانِبِ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنِ We called him from the right side of the Mount, (19:52), and He said in His address to Moses عليه‌السلام: فَاخْلَعْ نَعْلَيْكَ إِنَّكَ بِالْوادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوىً so take off your sandals; you are indeed in the holy valley of Tuwa. (20:12). He further said: نُودِيَ مِنْ شاطِئِ الْوادِ الْأَيْمَنِ فِي الْبُقْعَةِ الْمُبارَكَةِ مِنَ الشَّجَرَةِ he was called from the right bank of the valley in the blessed site from the tree: (28:30).

It has also been said that the intended meaning is any mountain in general; Allah the Exalted swore by it because of the various types of His blessings He deposited therein. The Exalted said: وَجَعَلَ فِيها رَواسِيَ مِنْ فَوْقِها وَبارَكَ فِيها He set in it firm mountains [rising] above it, and blessed it (41:10).

His saying, the Exalted: وَكِتابٍ مَسْطُورٍ فِي رَقٍّ مَنْشُورٍ by the Book inscribed on unrolled parchment, It is said that parchment (الرق) is anything written upon, and it is said to be paper, or paper made from skin. Unrolling (النشر) is the act of spreading out and dispersing.

Regarding the identity of this Book, it is said to be the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) in which Allah wrote what was, what is, and what will be, which the angels of heaven read. It is also said to be the records of deeds (صحائف الأعمال) read by the angelic keepers of deeds. Others say it is the Quran, which Allah wrote in the Preserved Tablet. It is also said to be the Torah, which used to be written on parchment and unrolled for reading.

In view of the preceding verse, the last opinion is the most appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْبَيْتِ الْمَعْمُورِ by the Edifice inhabited, It is said that the intended meaning is the Noble Kaaba, for it is the first house established for mankind and has remained inhabited (معمورا) since its establishment until this day of ours. The Exalted said: إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِي بِبَكَّةَ مُبارَكاً وَهُدىً لِلْعالَمِينَ Indeed the first house to be set up for mankind is the one at Bakkah, blessed and a guidance for all nations. (3:96).

In the transmitted narrations, the Inhabited House (البيت المعمور) is a house in heaven directly above the Kaaba, visited by the angels.

The use of the indefinite form for Book (كِتابٍ) is to intimate its self-sufficiency from being defined; thus, it is an indefiniteness that implies and necessitates definiteness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّقْفِ الْمَرْفُوعِ by the elevated roof, This refers to the heaven.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْبَحْرِ الْمَسْجُورِ by the surging sea, Al-Raghib said: al-Sajr (السجر) is the stirring up of fire. In al-Majma‘, it is said: al-Masjur (المسجور) means the filled; it is said: "I filled (سجرت) the oven," meaning I filled it with fire. The verse has been interpreted with both meanings. The first meaning is supported by His saying: وَإِذَا الْبِحارُ سُجِّرَتْ when the seas are set afire, (81:6), meaning kindled. It has been mentioned in the hadith that the seas will be kindled into fire on the Day of Resurrection. It is also said that the meaning is that their waters will recede by the kindling of fire within them.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّكَ لَواقِعٌ ما لَهُ مِنْ دافِعٍ Indeed your Lord’s punishment will surely come to pass; there is none to avert it. This is the response to the preceding oath. The punishment whose occurrence is announced is the punishment of the Day of Resurrection which Allah promised to the disbelieving deniers, as indicated by the following verse. In His saying: ما لَهُ مِنْ دافِعٍ there is none to avert it, there is an indication that it is part of the decreed judgment (القضاء المحتوم) whose occurrence is inevitable. The Exalted said: وَأَنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ لا رَيْبَ فِيها وَأَنَّ اللهَ يَبْعَثُ مَنْ فِي الْقُبُورِ and that the Hour is bound to come, there is no doubt about it, and that Allah will resurrect those who are in the graves. (22:7).

In His saying: عَذابَ رَبِّكَ your Lord’s punishment, by attributing the punishment to "the Lord" (الرب) suffixed with the second-person pronoun rather than saying "the punishment of Allah," there is a support for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله against those who deny his call, and a consolation for his soul that his Lord will not disgrace him on that day, as He said: يَوْمَ لا يُخْزِي اللهُ النَّبِيَّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَعَهُ the day Allah will not let down the Prophet and the faithful who are with him. (66:8).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَمُورُ السَّماءُ مَوْراً وَتَسِيرُ الْجِبالُ سَيْراً The day the sky will be in a state of commotion, and the mountains will be in motion. This is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) for His saying: إِنَّ عَذابَ رَبِّكَ لَواقِعٌ Indeed your Lord’s punishment will surely come to pass.

Commotion (المور) — according to al-Majma‘ — is the agitation of a thing by going back and forth, like the agitation of smoke before it vanishes. Close to this is the saying of al-Raghib: that it is a swift flowing.

In any case, it contains an allusion to the folding of the celestial world as the Exalted mentions in various places of His speech, such as His saying: إِذَا السَّماءُ انْفَطَرَتْ وَإِذَا الْكواكِبُ انْتَثَرَتْ When the sky is rent apart, and when the planets are scattered, (82:1-2), and His saying: يَوْمَ نَطْوِي السَّماءَ كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ The day We shall roll up the sky like the rolling of the scrolls for writings. (21:104), and His saying: وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ and the heavens will be folded up in His right hand. (39:67).

Similarly, His saying: وَتَسِيرُ الْجِبالُ سَيْراً and the mountains will be in motion, is an allusion to the earthquake of the Hour on the earth, which the Exalted mentions in various places of His speech, such as His saying: إِذا رُجَّتِ الْأَرْضُ رَجًّا وَبُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا فَكانَتْ هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا when the earth is shaken with a violent shaking, and the mountains are crumbled into pieces, and become scattered dust. (56:4-6), and His saying: وَسُيِّرَتِ الْجِبالُ فَكانَتْ سَراباً and the mountains will be set in motion and become a mirage. (78:20).

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالطُّورِ وَكِتابٍ مَسْطُورٍ — he said: "The Mount (الطور) is a mountain in Mount Sinai."

In al-Majma‘: وَالْبَيْتِ الْمَعْمُورِ — "It is a house in the fourth heaven directly above the Kaaba; the angels inhabit it through the worship they perform therein." This is narrated from Ibn Abbas and Mujahid. It is also narrated from the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام, who said: ويدخله كل يوم سبعون ألف ملك ثم لا يعودون إليه أبدا — "Seventy thousand angels enter it every day, and then they never return to it again."

I say: The fact that the Inhabited House (البيت المعمور) is a house in heaven around which the angels circumambulate is mentioned in several traditions from the paths of both groups (Sunni and Shia), though they differ regarding its location. In most of them, it is in the fourth heaven; in some, it is in the first heaven; and in others, the seventh.

In the same source: وَالسَّقْفِ الْمَرْفُوعِ — "It is the heaven," narrated from Ali عليه‌السلام.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: وَالسَّقْفِ الْمَرْفُوعِ — he said: "The heaven." وَالْبَحْرِ الْمَسْجُورِ — he said: "It will be set afire on the Day of Resurrection."

In al-Majma‘: وَالْبَحْرِ الْمَسْجُورِ — "Meaning the filled," narrated from Qatadah. It is also said: "It is the kindled and heated like an oven," narrated from Mujahid, al-Dahhak, al-Akhfash, and Ibn Zayd. Then it was said: "The seas will be heated on the Day of Resurrection and turned into fires—then they will burst into one another, and then burst into the Fire." A hadith has been narrated to this effect.

Ayahs

فَوَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (١١)
woe to the deniers on that day (11)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ (١٢)
—those who play around in vain talk, (12)
يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلَىٰ نَارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا (١٣)
the day when they will be shoved forcibly toward the fire of hell, [and told:] (13)
هَـٰذِهِ ٱلنَّارُ ٱلَّتِى كُنتُم بِهَا تُكَذِّبُونَ (١٤)
‘This is the Fire which you used to deny! (14)
أَفَسِحْرٌ هَـٰذَآ أَمْ أَنتُمْ لَا تُبْصِرُونَ (١٥)
Is this, then, [also] magic, or is it you who do not perceive? (15)
ٱصْلَوْهَا فَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ أَوْ لَا تَصْبِرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٦)
Enter it, and it will be the same for you whether you are patient or impatient. You are only being requited for what you used to do.’ (16)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَنَعِيمٍ (١٧)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and bliss, (17)
فَـٰكِهِينَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ وَوَقَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٨)
rejoicing because of what their Lord has given them, and that their Lord has saved them from the punishment of hell. (18)
كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٩)
[They will be told:] ‘Enjoy your food and drink, [as a reward] for what you used to do.’ (19)
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مَّصْفُوفَةٍ ۖ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ (٢٠)
They will be reclining on arrayed couches, and We will wed them to big-eyed houris. (20)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُم بِإِيمَـٰنٍ أَلْحَقْنَا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَمَآ أَلَتْنَـٰهُم مِّنْ عَمَلِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ ۚ كُلُّ ٱمْرِئٍۭ بِمَا كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ (٢١)
The faithful and their descendants who followed them in faith—We will make their descendants join them, and We will not stint anything from [the reward of] their deeds. Every person is hostage to what he has earned. (21)
وَأَمْدَدْنَـٰهُم بِفَـٰكِهَةٍ وَلَحْمٍ مِّمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٢٢)
We will provide them with fruits and meat, such as they desire. (22)
يَتَنَـٰزَعُونَ فِيهَا كَأْسًا لَّا لَغْوٌ فِيهَا وَلَا تَأْثِيمٌ (٢٣)
There they will pass from hand to hand a cup wherein there will be neither any vain talk nor sinful speech. (23)
۞ وَيَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ غِلْمَانٌ لَّهُمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لُؤْلُؤٌ مَّكْنُونٌ (٢٤)
They will be waited upon by their youths, as if they were guarded pearls. (24)
وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (٢٥)
They will turn to one another, questioning each other. (25)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِىٓ أَهْلِنَا مُشْفِقِينَ (٢٦)
They will say, ‘Indeed, aforetime, we used to be apprehensive about our families. (26)
فَمَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا وَوَقَىٰنَا عَذَابَ ٱلسَّمُومِ (٢٧)
But Allah showed us favour and He saved us from the punishment of the [infernal] miasma. (27)
إِنَّا كُنَّا مِن قَبْلُ نَدْعُوهُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْبَرُّ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٢٨)
We used to supplicate Him aforetime. Indeed He is the All-benign, the All-merciful.’ (28)

(Commentary)

The verses mention those upon whom this punishment—the occurrence and realization of which is beyond doubt—shall fall, and describe their state at that time. This is the primary objective of the Surah, as previously indicated. As for the description of the state of the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness (التقوى), the innate protective shield of the soul against divine displeasure — on that day, it is mentioned secondarily to reinforce the intended warning.

His saying, the Exalted: فَوَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُكَذِّبِينَ — "Woe to the deniers on that day!" (52:11). This is a derivation from what the preceding verses indicated regarding the certainty of the punishment occurring on the Day of Resurrection. That is, if the matter is as mentioned and there is no escape from the occurrence of the punishment, then woe to those upon whom it falls, and they are inevitably the deniers. Thus, the sentence indicates that the punished are the deniers by implication (الاستلزام), and indicates the attachment of "woe" to them by direct correspondence (المطابقة).

Alternatively, the estimation is: if the punishment is inevitably occurring, and it inevitably falls only upon the deniers—because they are the ones who disbelieve in Allah and deny the Day of Resurrection—then woe be to them on that day. Thus, that which indicates the attachment of the punishment to the deniers is His saying: عَذابَ رَبِّكَ — "the punishment of your Lord" (52:7), because the punishment of Allah only falls upon those who were called but did not respond and denied His call.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي خَوْضٍ يَلْعَبُونَ — "who play around in [vain] talk" (52:12). Vain talk (الخوض) is entering into falsehood of speech. Al-Raghib said: "Vain talk (الخوض) is originally wading into water and passing through it, and it is used metaphorically for affairs; most of its occurrences in the Qur'an refer to that which is blameworthy to enter into." End quote. The nunnation (التنوين) of indefiniteness in خَوْضٍ indicates a deleted adjective, i.e., "in a strange kind of vain talk."

Since engaging in falsehood of speech yields no true result but only an imaginary one embellished by the estimation (الوهم) for the one wading into it, He called it "play" (لعب) — and play (اللعب) consists of those actions that have nothing but an imaginary effect.

The meaning is: those who persist in a strange kind of vain talk, playing around by disputing the signs of Allah, denying them, and mocking them.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يُدَعُّونَ إِلى نارِ جَهَنَّمَ دَعًّا — "the day they will be pushed into the fire of hell with a [violent] push" (52:13). Pushing (الدع) is a forceful thrust. It appears that "the day" (يَوْمَ) is an explanation for His saying: "on that day" (يَوْمَئِذٍ).

His saying, the Exalted: هذِهِ النَّارُ الَّتِي كُنْتُمْ بِها تُكَذِّبُونَ — "[and told,] ‘This is the fire that you used to deny!’" (52:14). That is, it will be said to them: "This is the fire that you used to deny." What is meant by denying the fire is denying what the Prophets (peace be upon them) informed of through revelation from Allah regarding the existence of this fire and that the criminals would be punished by it. The sum of the meaning is: "This is the manifestation (مصداق) of what the Prophets informed you of, which you then denied."

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَسِحْرٌ هذا أَمْ أَنْتُمْ لا تُبْصِرُونَ — "Is this magic, then, or do you not see?" (52:15). This is a derivation from His saying: "This is the fire that you used to deny." The interrogation is for rebuke (الإنكار), derived for them; meaning, if this is that very fire which you used to deny, then this is not magic as you used to accuse the reports of the Prophets of being magic, nor is this an imaginary, superstitious matter as you used to utter, but rather a matter seen and witnessed by you. The verse carries the same meaning as His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يُعْرَضُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا عَلَى النَّارِ أَلَيْسَ هذا بِالْحَقِّ — "The day when the faithless are exposed to the Fire, [they will be asked,] ‘Is this not real?’" (46:34).

From the aforementioned meaning, it becomes clear that "or" (أَمْ) in His saying: أَمْ أَنْتُمْ لا تُبْصِرُونَ is conjunctive (متصلة). It has been said it is disjunctive (منقطعة), but this is not without far-fetchedness.

His saying, the Exalted: اصْلَوْها فَاصْبِرُوا أَوْ لا تَصْبِرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "Enter it! Then be patient or impatient; it is the same for you. You are only being requited for what you used to do" (52:16). Entering (الصلي) — with a fathah then a sukun — is to endure the heat of the fire. Thus, the meaning of "Enter it" is: endure the heat of the fire of Hell.

His saying: فَاصْبِرُوا أَوْ لا تَصْبِرُوا — "Then be patient or impatient" — is a derivation from the command to endure. The alternation between the command and the prohibition is a metaphor for the equality of the action and its abandonment. Therefore, He followed it with His saying: سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ — "it is the same for you" — meaning: this endurance is necessary for you and will not leave you, whether you are patient or not. Neither will patience remove the punishment from you or lighten it, nor will anguish and the lack of patience benefit you in any way.

His saying: سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ is the predicate (خبر) of a deleted subject (مبتدأ), i.e., "they both are equal." The use of the singular سَواءٌ is because it is originally a verbal noun (مصدر).

His saying: إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "You are only being requited for what you used to do" — is in the position of providing the rationale (التعليل) for what was mentioned regarding the necessity of the punishment and the equality of patience and anguish.

The meaning is: this evil requital is necessary for you and will not leave you because you are being requited by your very deeds that you used to do, and the relationship of the deed to its doer cannot be severed; thus, the punishment is inseparable from you. Or, it means you are only being requited by the consequences and the recompense of what you used to do.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَنَعِيمٍ — "Indeed the God-conscious will be in gardens and bliss" (52:17). The garden (الجنة) is the orchard whose trees cover and shade it, and bliss (النعيم) is abundant favor (النعمة). That is, those characterized by godwariness (تقوى) of Allah shall be, on that day, in gardens where they reside and surrounded by abundant favor.

His saying, the Exalted: فاكِهِينَ بِما آتاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ وَوَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ — "rejoicing in what their Lord has given them, and their Lord has saved them from the punishment of hell" (52:18). Fruit (الفاكهة) refers to fruit in general. It has been said it refers to fruit other than grapes and pomegranates. It is said tafakkaha and fakiha when one engages in jesting (الفكاهة), and tafakkaha and fakiha when one consumes fruit (الفاكهة). The verse has been interpreted with both meanings. It was said the meaning is: they converse about the bliss their Lord has given them. It was said the meaning is: they consume the fruits and produce their Lord has given them. It was said the meaning is: they take pleasure in the benevolence of their Lord, which returns to the first meaning. It was said it means they are joyful and delighted (فاكهين) with what their Lord has given them, which perhaps returns to the second meaning.

The repetition of "their Lord" (رَبُّهُمْ) in His saying: وَوَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ is to indicate increased care (العناية) for them.

His saying, the Exalted: كُلُوا وَاشْرَبُوا هَنِيئاً بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "[They will be told,] ‘Enjoy your food and drink, as a reward for what you used to do’" (52:19). That is, it will be said to them: "Eat and drink a wholesome eating and drinking," or "wholesome food and drink." Thus, "wholesome" (هَنِيئاً) is an adjective standing in the place of an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) or a direct object (مفعول به).

His saying: بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "for what you used to do" — is connected to His saying: "Eat and drink," or to His saying: "wholesome."

His saying, the Exalted: مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلى سُرُرٍ مَصْفُوفَةٍ وَزَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ — "reclining on couches ranged in rows, and We shall wed them to big-eyed houris" (52:20). Reclining (الاتكاء) is leaning upon a pillow or the like. Couches (السرر) is the plural of sarir. "Ranged in rows" (مصفوفة) is from saff, meaning lined up and joined one to another. The meaning is: reclining on pillows and cushions, sitting upon couches arranged in rows.

His saying: وَزَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ — "and We shall wed them to big-eyed houris." What is meant by "wedding" (التزويج) is pairing (القرن), meaning We have paired them with them, rather than marriage by contract (النكاح بالعقد). The evidence for this is its transitivity with the particle ba (ب), for "wedding" in the sense of marriage by contract is transitive by itself, as the Exalted said: زَوَّجْناكَها — "We wedded her to you" (33:37). So it has been said.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "The faithful and their descendants who followed them in faith—We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint them anything of their deeds" (52:21). It has been said that the difference between "following" (الاتباع) and "joining" (اللحوق)—while considering precedence and subsequence in both—is that in "following," a commonality is considered between the follower and the followed in the matter of following, unlike "joining," where the joiner does not necessarily share with the joined that which he joined him in.

Lata (لات) and alata (ألات) mean to diminish (نقص). Thus, the meaning of "We did not stint them" (ما ألتناهم) is: We did not diminish anything from their deeds by the act of joining.

The apparent sense of the verse is that it is in the context of bestowing favor (الامتنان). Thus, He, the Glorified, bestows favor upon those who believe that He will join with them their descendants who followed them in faith, so that their eyes may be comforted thereby. This is the context clue (القرينة) that the nunnation in "in faith" (بِإِيمانٍ) is for indefiniteness (التنكير) rather than for magnification (التعظيم).

The meaning is: they followed them with some degree of faith, even if it fell short of the level of their fathers' faith; for there would be no special favor bestowed if their faith were more perfect than their fathers' faith or equal to it.

The absolute use of "following" in faith is understood as the following of one from whom faith is valid in itself, by having reached the stage of being religiously accountable (التكليف). Thus, what is meant by "descendants" (الذرية) are the adult children who are accountable for faith. Therefore, the verse does not include small children who died before reaching maturity, and this does not contradict the fact that the small children of believers are legally ruled to be believers.

Unless, however, generality is derived from the indefiniteness of "faith," and the meaning becomes: "and their descendants followed them with some faith," whether it was faith in itself or faith according to the legal ruling.

Similarly, the bestowing of favor is a context clue that the pronoun in His saying: وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "and We will not stint them anything of their deeds" — refers to those who believe, just like the two pronouns in His saying: وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ — "and their descendants followed them." This is because His saying: "and We will not stint them anything of their deeds" is then intended to repel the illusion that a deficiency in reward would occur upon the decree of joining, which would contradict the bestowing of favor. It is well known that what contradicts the favor is the deficiency in the reward of the fathers to whom others are joined, not the descendants.

In summary, His saying: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا etc., is a new sentence (استئناف) in which the Exalted bestows favor upon those who believe, stating that He will join with them their children who followed them with some degree of faith, even if it was short of the level of their faith, so that their eyes may be comforted. Despite this, nothing of the reward of the fathers' deeds is diminished by the joining; rather, He gives them the like of what He gave the fathers, or in a manner where there is no crowding, according to what He knows best.

Regarding the meaning of the verse, there are other opinions not free from absurdity, such as the saying of some that His saying: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا is coordinated (معطوف) with "big-eyed houris," meaning: "We wedded them to big-eyed houris and to those who believe," so they enjoy the houris through marriage and the believers through companionship and society. And the saying of some that what is meant by "descendants" is small children only. And the saying of some that the two pronouns in وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ refer to the descendants, meaning: "We did not diminish the descendants anything of their deeds because of their being joined to their fathers; rather, We fulfill their deeds for them, whether good or evil, then We join them to their fathers."

His saying: كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ — "Every man is a hostage to what he has earned" (52:21). This is a rationale (تعليل) for His saying: "and We will not stint them anything of their deeds," according to what the context indicates. A pledge (الرهن), a hostage (الرهين), and a pledged object (المرهون) are what is placed as security for a debt, as mentioned by Al-Raghib. He said: "Since a pledge is conceived as being detained, it was used metaphorically for the detention of anything whatsoever." End quote.

Perhaps this metaphorical meaning is what is intended in the verse: a person is a pledge, held and preserved with Allah, the Glorified, for what he has earned of good or evil, until He fulfills for him the recompense of what he did, whether reward or punishment. If He were to diminish anything of his deed and not fulfill it for him, he would not be a hostage to what he earned, but rather a hostage to some of what he did, while another—such as his descendants joined to him—possessed the other part.

As for His saying, the Exalted: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ بِما كَسَبَتْ رَهِينَةٌ إِلَّا أَصْحابَ الْيَمِينِ — "Every soul is a hostage to what it has earned, except the People of the Right" (74:38-39), what is meant is its being a hostage to punishment on the Day of Resurrection, as witnessed by the context of what follows it in His saying: فِي جَنَّاتٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ عَنِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ — "in gardens, questioning the guilty" (74:40-41).

It has been said: what is meant is that a person is a hostage to his evil deed, as indicated by the verse of Surah al-Muddaththir mentioned just now, by the evidence of the exception of the "People of the Right." Thus, the verse—meaning His saying: كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ — is a parenthetical sentence (جملة معترضة) describing the attributes of the people of the Fire, interposed within the attributes of the people of Paradise.

The author of al-Kashshaf interpreted the verse as a type of metaphor, thereby removing the contradiction between the two verses. He said: "It is as if the soul of the servant is a pledge with Allah for the righteous deed he is demanded to perform, just as a man pledges his slave for a debt he owes. If he performs a righteous deed, he ransoms and frees it; otherwise, he destroys it." End quote.

You are well aware that what he mentioned alone does not justify the connection of the sentence—meaning His saying: "Every man is a hostage to what he has earned"—to what precedes it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمْدَدْناهُمْ بِفاكِهَةٍ وَلَحْمٍ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ — "We will provide them with such fruits and meat as they desire" (52:22). This is an explanation of some of their completions and enjoyments in Paradise, which were mentioned summarily in His previous saying: "Enjoy your food and drink," etc.

Providing (الإمداد) is to bring a thing time after time. It is used for good, just as madd (المد) is used for evil. The Exalted said: وَنَمُدُّ لَهُ مِنَ الْعَذابِ مَدًّا — "and We will prolong the punishment for him" (19:79).

The meaning is: We provide them with fruit and the meat they desire, a provision after provision and time after time, without interruption.

His saying, the Exalted: يَتَنازَعُونَ فِيها كَأْساً لا لَغْوٌ فِيها وَلا تَأْثِيمٌ — "There they will pass to one another a cup wherein is neither vain talk nor sin" (52:23). Contending (التنازع) for the cup is passing it to one another and gathering to consume it. A cup (الكأس) is a goblet, and the term "cup" is only applied when it contains a drink.

What is meant by "vain talk" (اللغو) is the vanity of speech that issues from drinkers of wine in the world. "Sinning" (التأثيم) is making a person a possessor of sin, which is also among the effects of wine in the world. The negation of vain talk and sinning is the context clue that what is meant by the cup they pass to one another is a cup of wine.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ غِلْمانٌ لَهُمْ كَأَنَّهُمْ لُؤْلُؤٌ مَكْنُونٌ — "They will be waited upon by their youths, as if they were hidden pearls" (52:24). What is meant is their going around them for service. Some have said: it was said "youths for them" (غِلْمانٌ لَهُمْ) in the indefinite form, and He did not say "their youths" (غلمانهم), lest it be imagined that what is meant by them are their servants who used to serve them in the world. Thus, they are like the houris, among the creations of Paradise, as if they were hidden pearls—stored away—in beauty, radiance, and purity.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ — "They will turn to one another, questioning each other" (52:25). That is, each of them asks the other about his state in the world and what it was that led him to Paradise and bliss.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِي أَهْلِنا مُشْفِقِينَ — "They will say, ‘Before this, we used to be apprehensive [of Allah’s punishment] among our folk’" (52:26). Al-Raghib said: "Apprehension (الإشفاق) is a care (عناية) mixed with fear, because the apprehensive one loves the one he is apprehensive for and fears what might befall him. The Exalted said: وَهُمْ مِنَ السَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ — 'and they are apprehensive of the Hour' (21:49). When it is made transitive with min (من), the meaning of fear in it is more apparent, and when it is made transitive with fi (في), the meaning of care in it is more apparent. The Exalted said: إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِي أَهْلِنا مُشْفِقِينَ." End quote.

The meaning is: We were in the world possessors of apprehension among our families, caring for their happiness and their salvation from the destruction of misguidance; so we consorted with them with beautiful conduct and walked among them by spreading sincere advice and the call to the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنَّ اللهُ عَلَيْنا وَوَقانا عَذابَ السَّمُومِ — "So Allah was gracious to us and He saved us from the punishment of the [scorching] Samum" (52:27). Bestowing favor (المن), according to what Al-Raghib mentioned, is to grant a heavy favor (النعمة الثقيلة). It can be by action, which is good, or by speech, which is ugly from other than Allah, the Exalted. The Exalted said: يَمُنُّونَ عَلَيْكَ أَنْ أَسْلَمُوا قُلْ لا تَمُنُّوا عَلَيَّ إِسْلامَكُمْ بَلِ اللهُ يَمُنُّ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْ هَداكُمْ لِلْإِيمانِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "They count it as a favor to you that they have embraced Islam. Say, ‘Do not count your embracing of Islam as a favor to me. Rather it is Allah who has done you a favor by guiding you to faith, if you are truthful’" (49:17).

The favor from Him, the Exalted, upon the people of Paradise is His granting them the success to enter it through mercy, and its completion is by protecting them from the punishment of the Samum.

The Samum (السموم) — according to what Al-Tabarsi mentioned — is the heat that enters the pores of the body, causing pain; from it comes the Samum wind.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا كُنَّا مِنْ قَبْلُ نَدْعُوهُ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْبَرُّ الرَّحِيمُ — "Before this we used to supplicate Him. Indeed, He is the All-benign, the All-merciful" (52:28). This is a rationale (تعليل) for His saying: "So Allah was gracious to us," etc., just as His saying: "Indeed, He is the All-benign, the All-merciful" is a rationale for it.

This verse, along with the two verses before it, indicates that these people used to call upon Allah in the world by affirming His monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — for worship and submission to His command, and they were apprehensive among their families, bringing them closer to the truth and distancing them from falsehood. This was the cause for Allah bestowing favor upon them with Paradise and protecting them from the punishment of the Samum. This was a cause for that only because He, the Exalted, is All-benign and All-merciful, so He acts benevolently toward whoever calls upon Him and has mercy upon him.

Thus, the three verses carry the same meaning as His saying: إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَتَواصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَواصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ — "Indeed man is in loss, except those who have faith and do righteous deeds, and enjoin one another to [follow] the truth, and enjoin one another to patience" (103:2-3).

The All-benign (البر) is among the Beautiful Names of Allah, and it is from al-birr (البر) meaning benevolence (الإحسان). Some have interpreted it as the Subtle (اللطيف).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Bakr, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ — "The faithful and their descendants who followed them in faith—We will unite their descendants with them." He said: "The children fell short of the deeds of the fathers, so the children were joined to the fathers so that their eyes might be comforted thereby."

I say: It was also narrated in al-Tawhid with its chain of narration to Abu Bakr al-Hadrami from him (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Qummi: My father narrated to me from Sulayman al-Daylami, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, the infants of our Shi‘a among the believers are raised by Fatimah (peace be upon her)." Regarding His saying: أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ, he said: "They are presented to their fathers on the Day of Resurrection."

I say: It was narrated in al-Majma‘ at the end of the hadith from him (peace be upon him) as a disconnected (مرسلا) report.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, who said: Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "When an infant among the infants of the believers dies, a caller cries out in the kingdom of the heavens and the earth: 'Behold, so-and-so son of so-and-so has died.' If his parents or one of them, or some of his family members from the believers have already died, he is handed over to them to nourish him. Otherwise, he is handed over to Fatimah to nourish him until his parents or one of them, or some of his family members from the believers arrive, then she hands him over to them."

In al-Faqih: In a narration of al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from ‘Ali, from al-Halabi, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed, Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, entrusted Ibrahim and Sarah with the infants of the believers; they nourish them from a tree in Paradise that has teats like the teats of cows in a palace of pearl. When it is the Day of Resurrection, they are clothed, perfumed, and presented to their fathers; so they are kings in Paradise with their fathers. This is the saying of Allah, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ."

In al-Majma‘, Zadhan narrated from ‘Ali (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "Indeed, the believers and their children are in Paradise," then he recited this verse.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bazzar and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn ‘Abbas, who attributed it to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), saying: "Indeed, Allah raises the descendants of the believer to him in his rank, even if they were below him in deeds," then he recited: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ. He said: "And We did not diminish the fathers because of what We gave the children."

In the same source, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Ibn ‘Abbas that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: "When a man enters Paradise, he asks about his parents, his descendants, and his children. It is said: 'They did not reach your rank or your deeds.' He says: 'O Lord, I worked for myself and for them.' So it is ordered that they be joined to him." And Ibn ‘Abbas recited: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ the verse.

I say: The verse does not include the "parents" mentioned in the hadith. More suitable to indicate that is what the Exalted mentioned in the supplication of the angels: رَبَّنا وَأَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ وَمَنْ صَلَحَ مِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَأَزْواجِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ — "Our Lord, admit them into the Gardens of Eden, which You have promised them, along with whoever is righteous among their forebears, their spouses, and their descendants" (40:8).

In Tafsir al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: لا لَغْوٌ فِيها وَلا تَأْثِيمٌ, he said: "There is no singing or obscenity in Paradise, and the believer drinks and does not sin." Regarding وَأَقْبَلَ بَعْضُهُمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ يَتَساءَلُونَ, he said: "In Paradise."

Ayahs

فَذَكِّرْ فَمَآ أَنتَ بِنِعْمَتِ رَبِّكَ بِكَاهِنٍ وَلَا مَجْنُونٍ (٢٩)
So admonish. By your Lord’s blessing, you are not a soothsayer, nor mad. (29)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ شَاعِرٌ نَّتَرَبَّصُ بِهِۦ رَيْبَ ٱلْمَنُونِ (٣٠)
Do they say, ‘[He is] a poet, for whom we await a fatal accident’? (30)
قُلْ تَرَبَّصُوا۟ فَإِنِّى مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْمُتَرَبِّصِينَ (٣١)
Say, ‘Wait! I too am waiting along with you.’ (31)
أَمْ تَأْمُرُهُمْ أَحْلَـٰمُهُم بِهَـٰذَآ ۚ أَمْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ (٣٢)
Is it their intellect which prompts them to [say] this, or are they a rebellious lot? (32)
أَمْ يَقُولُونَ تَقَوَّلَهُۥ ۚ بَل لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٣٣)
Do they say, ‘He has improvised it [himself]?’ Rather, they have no faith! (33)
فَلْيَأْتُوا۟ بِحَدِيثٍ مِّثْلِهِۦٓ إِن كَانُوا۟ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٤)
Let them bring a discourse like it, if they are truthful. (34)
أَمْ خُلِقُوا۟ مِنْ غَيْرِ شَىْءٍ أَمْ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُونَ (٣٥)
Were they created from nothing? Or are they [their own] creators? (35)
أَمْ خَلَقُوا۟ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۚ بَل لَّا يُوقِنُونَ (٣٦)
Did they create the heavens and the earth? Rather, they have no certainty! (36)
أَمْ عِندَهُمْ خَزَآئِنُ رَبِّكَ أَمْ هُمُ ٱلْمُصَۣيْطِرُونَ (٣٧)
Do they possess the treasuries of your Lord? Or do they control [them]? (37)
أَمْ لَهُمْ سُلَّمٌ يَسْتَمِعُونَ فِيهِ ۖ فَلْيَأْتِ مُسْتَمِعُهُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٨)
Do they have a ladder [leading up to heaven] whereby they eavesdrop? If so, let their eavesdropper produce a manifest authority. (38)
أَمْ لَهُ ٱلْبَنَـٰتُ وَلَكُمُ ٱلْبَنُونَ (٣٩)
Does He have daughters while you have sons? (39)
أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ أَجْرًا فَهُم مِّن مَّغْرَمٍ مُّثْقَلُونَ (٤٠)
Do you ask them for a reward, so that they are [wary of] being weighed down with debt? (40)
أَمْ عِندَهُمُ ٱلْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ (٤١)
Do they have [access to] the Unseen, which they write down? (41)
أَمْ يُرِيدُونَ كَيْدًا ۖ فَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْمَكِيدُونَ (٤٢)
Do they seek to outmaneuver [Allah]? But it is the faithless who are the outmaneuvered ones! (42)
أَمْ لَهُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٤٣)
Do they have any god other than Allah? Clear is Allah of any partners that they may ascribe [to Him]! (43)
وَإِن يَرَوْا۟ كِسْفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ سَاقِطًا يَقُولُوا۟ سَحَابٌ مَّرْكُومٌ (٤٤)
Were they to see a fragment falling from the sky, they would say, ‘A cumulous cloud.’ (44)

(Commentary)

After informing of the punishment that will occur on the Day of Resurrection and that it will surely strike the deniers, while the God-conscious (المتقون) — those who guard themselves against evil through piety and awareness of Allah — will be in gardens and bliss, their eyes delighted, He commanded the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to proceed with his mission and reminder. This indicates that he is fit to establish the true call, and there is no excuse for these deniers in their rejection of him and their rebuffing of his invitation.

Thus, He negated all conceivable excuses for them, which consist of sixteen matters. One half of these pertains to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); had any of them been true of him, it would have stripped him of his eligibility to be followed and would have been an obstacle to accepting his word—such as his being a soothsayer, a madman, a poet, or a fabricator (متقول) forging lies against Allah, or his asking for a reward for his call. The other half pertains to the deniers themselves, such as the notion that they were created from nothing, or that they are the creators, or that their intellects command them to deny, among other things. Despite this, the verses are not devoid of severe rebuke for their denial.

His saying, the Exalted: فَذَكِّرْ فَمَا أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِكَاهِنٍ وَلَا مَجْنُونٍ "So proclaim the [divine] message: by your Lord’s blessing you are neither a soothsayer nor mad" (52:29). This is a derivation from what preceded of the emphasized report regarding the occurrence of the divine punishment on the Day of Resurrection, and that it will envelop the deniers, while the God-conscious will be in protection from it, enjoying the bliss of Paradise.

The verse is in the sense of saying: If this is the truth, then remind, for you only remind and warn with the truth, and you are not, as they charge you, a soothsayer or a madman.

The qualification of the negation with His saying, بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ "by your Lord’s blessing," conveys the sense of bestowing a favor specifically upon the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This specific favor is not merely due to the absence of soothsaying and madness—for most people possess this quality—but rather due to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being clothed in the specific blessing belonging to him, which prevents these attributes, such as soothsaying, madness, and others, from befalling him.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ شَاعِرٌ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِهِ رَيْبَ الْمَنُونِ "Do they say, ‘[He is] a poet; we await for him some calamity of fortune’?" (52:30). The word أَمْ is disjunctive (منقطعة). التربص means waiting. In Majma‘ al-Bayan, it says: التربص is waiting for something to change from one state to its opposite; المنون refers to fate and death; and الريب is anxiety and agitation. Thus, ريب المنون is the agitation of death.

The summary of the meaning is: Rather, they say he—meaning the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—is a poet for whom we await death, until he dies, his mention is extinguished, and his trace is forgotten, so we may be relieved of him.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ تَرَبَّصُوا فَإِنِّي مَعَكُمْ مِنَ الْمُتَرَبِّصِينَ "Say, ‘Wait! I too am waiting with you’" (52:31). The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to tell them to wait, just as they were satisfied with that for themselves. This is a command of threat; meaning: wait as you see fit for yourselves, for there is a matter whose occurrence ought to be expected, and I am waiting for it just as you are, but it shall be against you, not for you—and that is your destruction and the falling of punishment upon you.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ تَأْمُرُهُمْ أَحْلَامُهُمْ بِهَذَا "Do their intellects prompt them to [say] this?" (52:32). الأحلام is the plural of حلم, which means the intellect (العقل). The word أَمْ is disjunctive, and the speech implies an interrogative. The demonstration بِهَذَا (to this) refers to what they say to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and their awaiting his end.

The meaning is: Rather, do their intellects command them to say what they say and to await his death? For what intellect would repel the truth with such vanities?

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ "Or are they a rebellious people?" (52:32). That is, their intellects did not command them to do this; rather, they are rebellious (طاغون), and their rebellion (طغيان) is what drove them to this.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ تَقَوَّلَهُ بَلْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ "Do they say, ‘He has fabricated it’? Rather, they do not have faith" (52:33). It is said in al-Majma‘: التقول is to laboriously devise speech, and it is only used in the context of lying. The meaning is: Rather, they say: He has invented the Quran and attributed it to Allah falsely and as a fabrication. Nay, they do not believe, so they hurl this slander at him.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلْيَأْتُوا بِحَدِيثٍ مِثْلِهِ إِنْ كَانُوا صَادِقِينَ "Let them bring a discourse like it, if they are truthful" (52:34). This is a response to their saying, تَقَوَّلَهُ (he fabricated it), on the grounds that if it were the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), it would be human speech similar to all other speech, and other speech would be similar to it. Thus, it would be possible for them to bring a discourse like it. So let them bring a discourse like it if they are truthful in their claim of fabrication. Rather, it is divine speech upon which the signs of inimitability (الإعجاز) are manifest, such that humans are incapable of producing its like. The discussion regarding the aspects of the Quran's inimitability was previously detailed in the commentary on Surah al-Baqarah, verse 23.

It is possible to take the verse as a refutation of all that preceded of their reported sayings—that he is a soothsayer, a madman, a poet, or a fabricator—because the inability of humans to produce its like insists that it must be the speech of Allah, the Glorified; however, the former explanation is more apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ خُلِقُوا مِنْ غَيْرِ شَيْءٍ أَمْ هُمُ الْخَالِقُونَ "Were they created from nothing? Or are they the creators?" (52:35). The word شَيْءٍ (thing) is indefinite, implying an attribute suitable to the context; the estimation being: from nothing other than that from which other humans were created.

The meaning is: Rather, were these deniers created from something other than that from which other humans were created, such that it was appropriate to send a messenger, call to the truth, and be characterized by servitude (العبودية) to Him, the Exalted? Thus, no obligation (تكليف) would attach to them, nor would any command or prohibition be directed toward them, nor would their deeds be followed by reward or punishment, because they were created from something other than what others were created from.

There are other opinions regarding the meaning of this sentence.

It was said: The meaning is, were they brought into existence and proportioned with this exquisite proportion without a proportioner and creator? Thus, they have no need for a creator to manage their affairs.

It was said: The meaning is, were they created from something non-living? Thus, they are neither commanded nor prohibited, like inanimate objects.

It was said: The meaning is, were they created without a cause, or for no purpose of reward and punishment? Thus, for that reason, they do not listen.

It was said: The meaning is, were they created in vain (باطلاً), such that they are not held to account, nor commanded, nor prohibited?

The meaning we first presented is closer to the wording of the verse and more comprehensive.

And His saying: أَمْ هُمُ الْخَالِقُونَ "Or are they the creators?" (52:35). That is, creators of themselves? Thus, they would not be created by Allah, the Glorified, such that He would be their Lord and manage their affairs through command and prohibition.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ خَلَقُوا السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بَلْ لَا يُوقِنُونَ "Did they create the heavens and the earth? Rather, they have no certainty" (52:36). That is, did they create the world such that they would be lords and gods, too exalted to be enslaved or tasked with the obligations of servitude? Rather, they are a people who have no certainty (يوقنون).

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزَائِنُ رَبِّكَ أَمْ هُمُ الْمُصَيْطِرُونَ "Do they possess the treasuries of your Lord? Or are they the controllers?" (52:37). That is, rather, do they possess the treasuries of your Lord such that they may grant prophethood to whom they wish and withhold it from whom they wish, thereby denying you prophethood and messengership?

And His saying: أَمْ هُمُ الْمُصَيْطِرُونَ "Or are they the controllers?" (52:37). السيطرة (control)—and sometimes its sin is turned into a sad—means dominance and subjugation. The meaning is: Rather, are they the dominant ones, prevailing over Allah, the Glorified, such that they can strip from you what Allah has provided you of prophethood and messengership?

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ لَهُمْ سُلَّمٌ يَسْتَمِعُونَ فِيهِ فَلْيَأْتِ مُسْتَمِعُهُمْ بِسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ "Do they have a ladder whereby they eavesdrop? Then let their eavesdropper bring a manifest proof" (52:38). السلم is a ladder with rungs used as a means to ascend to high places. The word "listening" (الاستماع) here implies the meaning of ascending. السلطان means an argument or proof (البرهان).

The meaning is: Rather, do they have a ladder by which they ascend to the heaven, and by ascending it, they listen to the revelation, taking what is revealed to them and rejecting everything else? Then let their listener—meaning the one among them claiming to have listened—bring a manifest proof (حجة).

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ لَهُ الْبَنَاتُ وَلَكُمُ الْبَنُونَ "Does He have daughters while you have sons?" (52:39). It was said: In this is a mockery of their intellects, as they attributed to Him, the Exalted, that which they themselves disdained.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ تَسْأَلُهُمْ أَجْراً فَهُمْ مِنْ مَغْرَمٍ مُثْقَلُونَ "Do you ask them for a reward, so that they are overburdened by a debt?" (52:40). Al-Raghib said: الغرم (debt/loss) is the damage that befalls a person in his wealth without any crime or betrayal on his part. الإثقال (overburdening) is to load with a heavy weight, which is a metaphor for hardship.

The meaning is: Rather, do you ask them for a reward for delivering your message, such that they find it difficult to bear the loss that befalls them by paying the reward?

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ عِنْدَهُمُ الْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ "Do they possess [the knowledge of] the Unseen, so they write [it] down?" (52:41). Some have mentioned that the Unseen (الغيب) refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) in which the unseen matters are written. The meaning is: Rather, do they possess the Preserved Tablet, writing from it and informing the people of it? Thus, what they have informed about you would be from the Unseen about which there is no doubt.

It was said: The Unseen refers to the knowledge of the Unseen, and writing refers to establishing. The meaning is: Rather, do they possess the knowledge of the Unseen, so they establish what they have known as a law for the people, who must then obey them in what they have established? It was also said: يكتبون (they write) means they judge.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يُرِيدُونَ كَيْداً فَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هُمُ الْمَكِيدُونَ "Do they seek to [concoct] a stratagem? But it is the faithless who are the victims of [their own] stratagem" (52:42). الكيد (stratagem) is a type of trickery, as mentioned by al-Raghib. In al-Majma‘: الكيد is plotting (المكر), and it was said: it is doing that which necessitates rage in secret.

The apparent context is that what is meant by their stratagem is their plotting against the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) through what they charged him with—soothsaying, madness, poetry, and fabrication—so that people would turn away from him and distance themselves, thereby nullifying his call and extinguishing his light. This is a stratagem from them and a plot against their own souls, as they deprive them of eternal happiness and of embarking upon the straight path (صراط الحق) through that. Rather, it is a stratagem from Allah by cutting off success (التوفيق) from them and sealing their hearts.

It was said: The stratagem they intend is what occurred from them regarding him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the Assembly Hall (دار الندوة), and the "faithless" (الذين كفروا) mentioned are the deniers, the companions of the Assembly Hall. Allah turned their stratagem against themselves and they were killed on the day of Badr. According to this, the speech is an informing of the Unseen, as the Surah was revealed long before that. This is far from the context.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ لَهُمْ إِلَهٌ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ "Do they have a god other than Allah? Immaculate is Allah of what they ascribe [to Him] as partners!" (52:43). For if they had a god other than Allah, he would be their creator and the manager of their affairs; thus, they would be independent of Allah, the Glorified, and of responding to the call of His Messenger, and their god would help them and repel from them the punishment of Allah with which the deniers were promised and with which His Messenger warned them.

And His saying: سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ "Immaculate is Allah of what they ascribe [to Him] as partners!" (52:43) is a sanctification of Him, the Exalted, from having a partner as they claim. The ما in His saying عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ is infinitive (مصدرية); meaning: Glory be to Him from their shirk (شركهم).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ يَرَوْا كِسْفاً مِنَ السَّمَاءِ سَاقِطاً يَقُولُوا سَحَابٌ مَرْكُومٌ "Even if they were to see a fragment of the sky falling down, they would say, ‘A mass of clouds!’" (52:44). الكسف is a piece. المركوم is that which is piled up, one part falling upon another.

The meaning is: Their disbelief and their persistence in denying the true call has reached a point where, if they were to see a piece of the sky falling upon them, they would say it is a mass of clouds, having nothing to do with the sign of punishment. This is like His saying: وَلَوْ فَتَحْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ بَاباً مِنَ السَّمَاءِ فَظَلُّوا فِيهِ يَعْرُجُونَ لَقَالُوا إِنَّمَا سُكِّرَتْ أَبْصَارُنَا "Even if We open a gate to the sky for them, and they were to keep ascending through it, they would surely say, ‘Our eyes have been dazzled!’" (15:14-15).

Ayahs

فَذَرْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ يُصْعَقُونَ (٤٥)
So leave them until they encounter the day when they will be thunderstruck, (45)
يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٤٦)
the day when their guile will not avail them in any way, nor will they be helped. (46)
وَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ عَذَابًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٧)
Indeed there is a punishment besides that for those who do wrong, but most of them do not know. (47)
وَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا ۖ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ (٤٨)
So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord, for indeed you fare before Our eyes. And celebrate the praise of your Lord when you rise [at dawn], (48)
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَإِدْبَـٰرَ ٱلنُّجُومِ (٤٩)
and also glorify Him during the night and at the receding of the stars. (49)

(Commentary)

These verses conclude the Surah, commanding the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to leave those deniers to their own devices and not to concern himself with their state, but rather to remain patient for the judgment of his Lord and to glorify Him with His praise. Amidst this, there is a reiteration of the threat against them regarding the impending punishment (عذاب واقع) mentioned at the beginning of the Surah—a punishment that none can avert—while adding a further threat of another punishment, lesser than that, for those who do wrong.

His saying, the Exalted: فَذَرْهُمْ حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي فِيهِ يُصْعَقُونَ So leave them alone until they face their day in which they will be struck by a thunderbolt (45). The word ذرهم (leave them) is a command meaning "abandon them"; it is a verb whose conjugated forms are only used in the future and the imperative. The word يصعقون (they will be struck/faint) is derived from al-is’aq (الإصعاق), meaning to be put to death, though it is also said to be from al-sa’q (الصعق), also meaning to be put to death.

When the Glorified warned the deniers of his call about an impending punishment (عذاب واقع) in which there is no doubt, and then refuted all the excuses they offered—or those they might hypothetically offer—and mentioned that they are so persistent in falsehood that even if they were to witness the clearest sign of truth, they would interpret it away and reject it, He commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to leave them to their own state. This is a metaphorical threat (تهديد كنائي) implying that the punishment will encompass them while they are in this condition.

What is meant by "the day in which they will be struck by a thunderbolt" is the Day of the Blowing of the Trumpet, when everyone in the heavens and the earth will be struck down; this is among the portents of the Hour (أشراط الساعة). The Exalted said: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon (39:68).

This meaning is supported by His saying in the following verse: يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْئاً وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ the day when their guile will not avail them anything, nor will they be helped (46). Indeed, the negation of the efficacy of guile and help is among the specific characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, the day when all secondary causes (الأسباب) fail and the command on that day belongs solely to Allah.

An objection has been raised that only those who are alive on the Day of the Blowing will be struck down, whereas these deniers will not be alive then. The response is that everyone in this world among the living, as well as those in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — the state between death and resurrection — among the dead, will be struck down. If they are not in this world, they are in the intermediate realm (البرزخ).

Furthermore, it is possible that the pronoun in يُصْعَقُونَ (they will be struck) refers to those living at that time, and the threat is directed toward the punishment occurring on that day, rather than the specific act of being struck down (الصعقة) itself.

It has been said that it refers to the Day of Badr, but this is far-fetched. It has also been said that it refers to the day of death, but this does not align with the context, which clearly points to the threat mentioned at the beginning of the Surah—namely, the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, not the punishment of the day of death.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا عَذاباً دُونَ ذلِكَ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ Indeed for those who do wrong there is a punishment besides that, but most of them do not know (47). It is not improbable that what is meant here is the punishment of the grave (عذاب القبر). His saying, وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (but most of them do not know), suggests that among them are those who do know this, yet they persist in their faithlessness (الكفر) and denial out of sheer obstinacy (عناداً). It has also been said that it refers to the Day of Badr, but the conclusion of the verse does not fit that interpretation as well.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا So submit patiently to the judgment of your Lord, for indeed you are under Our eyes (48). This is conjoined to His saying, فَذَرْهُمْ (so leave them). The apparent context suggests that the "judgment" (الحكم) refers to His judgment regarding the deniers—granting them respite (الإمهال), dictating their term (الإملاء), and sealing their hearts—and His judgment regarding the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to call to the truth despite the harm he endures for the sake of Allah. Thus, the meaning of His saying, فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا (for indeed you are under Our eyes), is: "You are within Our sight; We see you such that nothing of your state is hidden from Us, and We are never heedless of you." The justification of patience with this phrase serves to emphasize the command to be patient and to intensify the address.

It has also been said that the meaning of فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا is: "You are under Our protection and guardianship." Thus, "the eye" (العين) is a metaphor (مجاز) for protection (الحفظ). Perhaps the former meaning is more suited to the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَإِدْبارَ النُّجُومِ and celebrate the praise of your Lord when you rise, and glorify Him in the night and at the receding of the stars (48-49). The preposition bi (ب) in بِحَمْدِ (with praise) denotes accompaniment; that is, "glorify your Lord and declare His transcendence (نزهه) while such glorification is coupled with His praise."

Regarding His saying, حِينَ تَقُومُ (when you rise), it has been said to mean rising from sleep. It has also been said to mean rising from the midday nap (القائلة), referring to the Noon Prayer (صلاة الظهر). Others say it means rising from a gathering, or every instance of rising, or rising for the obligatory prayer, or rising for every prayer. It has also been said to refer to the two units (ركعتان) before the obligatory Dawn Prayer. These are seven opinions as mentioned by al-Tabarsi.

His saying, وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ (and glorify Him in the night), means: "And during the night, glorify your Lord therein." This refers to the Night Prayer (صلاة الليل), though it has also been said to refer to the Maghrib and Isha prayers.

His saying, وَإِدْبارَ النُّجُومِ (and at the receding of the stars), is said to mean the time when the stars recede—that is, when they disappear due to the light of dawn. This refers to the two units (ركعتان) before the obligatory Dawn Prayer. It has also been said to refer to the obligatory Dawn Prayer itself, or to glorifying the Exalted morning and evening without being heedless of His remembrance.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ (and celebrate the praise of your Lord when you rise), he said: "For the Night Prayer." Regarding فَسَبِّحْهُ (and glorify Him), he said: "The Night Prayer."

I say: This meaning is also narrated in Majma’ al-Bayan from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليهما السلام).

In the same source, through his chain of narration from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام), he said: "أدبار السجود (after the prostrations) refers to four units (ركعات) after Maghrib, and إدبار النجوم (the receding of the stars) refers to two units (ركعتان) before the Dawn Prayer."

I say: The latter part of this is narrated in al-Majma’ from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليهما السلام), and by al-Qummi through his chain from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام).

It has been reported through Sunni channels in several narrations that whenever the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) rose from his gathering, he would glorify Allah and praise Him, saying: "It is an expiation for the gathering (كفارة المجلس)." However, these do not clearly appear to be an exegesis (تفسير) of the verse itself.
